coredata.dc:title: One-pot functionalization of carbon dots with ecPis-4s antimicrobial peptide

coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of nanobiostructures (CD@ecPis-4s) composed of carbon dots (CD) covalently bound to the antimicrobial peptide ecPis-4s is reported. Nanoparticle functionalization was achieved by pre-activating the oxygenated functional groups using 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDAC) and ethyl (hydroxyimino)cyanoacetate in an aqueous medium. The obtained nanobiostructures were characterized by several techniques, including FTIR and 1H NMR spectroscopies. The functionalization does not compromise the optical properties of CD. The polypeptide moiety of the peptide-decorated nanoparticles retains the active α-helical conformation and lytic activity in the presence of anionic phospholipid vesicles as observed in the circular dichroism and calcein release experiments. Consequently, the CD@ecPis-4s nanobiostructures present similar activity against Gram-positive (S. aureus and S. agalactiae) and Gram-negative (E. coli and P. aeruginosa) bacteria strains when compared to the free peptide. To our knowledge, this is the first report on carbon dots containing a covalently bound antimicrobial peptide. Thereby, in an era of increasing antimicrobial resistance, the synthesis and investigations performed in this work led to materials with potential for monitoring, in loco, bacterial contamination via exposure to UV radiation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of nanobiostructures (CD@ecPis-4s) composed of carbon dots (CD) covalently bound to the antimicrobial peptide ecPis-4s is reported. Nanoparticle functionalization was achieved by pre-activating the oxygenated functional groups using 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDAC) and ethyl (hydroxyimino)cyanoacetate in an aqueous medium. The obtained nanobiostructures were characterized by several techniques, including FTIR and 1H NMR spectroscopies. The functionalization does not compromise the optical properties of CD. The polypeptide moiety of the peptide-decorated nanoparticles retains the active α-helical conformation and lytic activity in the presence of anionic phospholipid vesicles as observed in the circular dichroism and calcein release experiments. Consequently, the CD@ecPis-4s nanobiostructures present similar activity against Gram-positive (S. aureus and S. agalactiae) and Gram-negative (E. coli and P. aeruginosa) bacteria strains when compared to the free peptide. To our knowledge, this is the first report on carbon dots containing a covalently bound antimicrobial peptide. Thereby, in an era of increasing antimicrobial resistance, the synthesis and investigations performed in this work led to materials with potential for monitoring, in loco, bacterial contamination via exposure to UV radiation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of novel antimicrobial agents has become even more needed due to the resistance acquired by pathogens to conventional drugs [1]. Surfaces and materials with antimicrobial properties represent important strategies for facing multidrug-resistant bacteria to ensure food safety and health care [2,3,3–5]. Therefore, nanostructures with antimicrobial properties have been reported as alternative biomaterials [6], since they can be applied directly on surfaces or even used as filling of polymeric materials improving mechanical properties and ensuring protection against bacterial infection [7,8]. Recently, research on hybrid nanoparticles functionalized with antimicrobial peptides have proved consistent enhancements in the activity and dispersion capacity of the new materials [1,8–11]. Torres et al. prepared a fibrous-like alumina nanoparticle functionalized with 3-aminopropyltrietoxysilane for covalent binding of the antimicrobial peptide BP100. Although the obtained nanobiostructure has been characterized with relatively low degree of peptide functionalization, its antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli and Salmonella typhimurium strains was considerably higher than the activity of the free peptide [10]. Chaudhari et al. prepared silver-coated single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs-Ag) decorated with the antimicrobial peptide TP359. The obtained nanoparticles showed considerable lower minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) in antibacterial assays when compared to SWCNTs-Ag and they were virtually non-toxic to the eukaryotic cells [11]. The authors suggest the increase of the antimicrobial activities of peptide-decorated nanoparticles are related to the high local concentration of the peptides in the membrane surface, since a large number of molecules covalently bound to the nanoparticles simultaneously achieve the membrane surface.

Carbon dots (CD) have been extensively studied due their potential biomedical applications, mainly at analytical sensors and bioimaging devices as well as in the development of polymeric nanocomposites for smart artificial muscle/tissue and wound dressing applications [12–14]. CD are considered a quasi-spherical fluorescent carbon nanomaterial with less than 10 nm in size. These nanostructures were first observed by Xu et al., in 2004 during the electrophoretic purification of SWCNT [15]. Besides their distinct electronic and optical properties, they present low toxicity and high biocompatibility for incorporation in different materials. CD present a heterogeneous chemical surface, usually formed by oxygenated functional groups, which constitute a chemical platform suitable for distinct functionalization methods aiming the optimization of properties for a variety of applications, including as antioxidant agents [16].

Some works in the literature have discussed the functionalization of CD with biological molecules, especially with DNA [17]. Nevertheless, some reports on the conjugation of CD to peptides are also found. Xia et al. prepared carbon dots by hydrothermal pyrolysis of sucrose conjugated with amyloid-beta 42 peptides, used as a potential fluorescent probe for amyloid imaging [18]. Lin et al. prepared peptide-functionalized CD, based on a stable π-π stacking interactions. The product exhibited negligible cytotoxicity to neuroblastoma cells up to 50 μg/mL, and it was used to obtain a sensitive and selective sensor of Ca2+ ions for biological applications [19]. Song et al. prepared CD functionalized with trans-activator of transcription (TAT) peptide and used as nuclear-targeted fluorescent probes for both one- and two-photon live-cell fluorescence imaging [20].

In this work, we present a green reaction method for the functionalization of carbon dots with the antimicrobial peptide piscidin ecPis-4s, a 22-residue cationic peptide (FFRHIKSFWKGAKAIFRGARQG-NH2) originally isolated from the fish species Epinephelus coioides [21]. In addition to low hemolytic activity, this peptide showed activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, as well as against fungi [21,22]. To our knowledge, this is the first report on carbon dots containing a covalently bound antimicrobial peptide. The optical properties of CD, i.e., molar absorptivity and quantum yield fluorescence, as well as the antimicrobial and antioxidant activities of the nanobiostructures were determined. In addition, investigation of conformational preferences and lytic activity of the peptide-decorated carbon dots are presented.

Cotton was purchased from the local market. Nitric acid (Neon), sulfuric acid (Dinâmica), hydrochloric acid (Vetec), sodium hydroxide (Cromato Chemical products), sodium carbonate (Dinâmica), dichloromethane (ISOFAR), quinine sulfate (Sigma-Aldrich), 1- were acquired from Luxembourg Bio Technologies (Ness Ziona, Israel). Ethyl-3-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDAC), ethyl(hydroxyimino)cyanoacetate (OxymaPure®), cloranfenicol, 2,2-diphenyl-1-picryl-hydrazyl-hydrate (DPPH) and Fmoc amino acid derivatives were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Gallic acid was purchased from Impex, Labimpex (Wood Dale, IL, United States). Fmoc Rink-amide® resin was obtained from NovaBiochem® (Darmstadt, Germany). Fmoc-amino acid derivatives, 1,3-diisopropylcarbodiimide (DIC), 1-hydroxybenzotriazole (HOBt), 4-methylpiperidine (PIPE), trifluoroacetic acid (TFA, analytical and HPLC grades) and triisopropylsilane (TIS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich® (San Louis, MO, USA). Dichloromethane (DCM), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich® (San Louis, MO, USA). 1-palmitoyl-2-oleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (POPC), 1-palmitoyl-2-oleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoglycerol (POPG) and polycarbonate membranes 100 nm were purchased from Avanti Polar Lipids®, Inc. (Alabaster, AL, USA).

EcPis-4s (FFRHIKSFWKGAKAIFRGARQG-NH2) peptide was obtained by solid-phase synthesis via 9-fluorenylmethyloxycarbonyl (Fmoc) strategy [23] on a Rink-amide resin (substitution degree: 0.63 mmol g−1). All synthesis steps were carried out in a polypropylene syringe (5.0 mL) adapted with a porous polyurethane filter. The coupling steps were carried out using the 1,3-diisopropylcarbodiimide (DIC) and 1-hydroxybenzotriazole (HOBt) activators in DMF:DCM (1:1) under stirring (300 rpm) for 150 min. Deprotection reactions were performed with 20% (v/v) 4-methylpiperidine(PIPE) in DMF for 30 min (two steps of 15 min, 300 rpm). After each deprotection and coupling step, the resin was alternately washed with DMF, DCM, and isopropyl alcohol (IPA), three times each one in this order. The cleavage reaction was performed with a trifluoroacetic acid (TFA):triisopropylsilane:H2O (95.0:2.5:2.5, v:v:v) solution during 2.0 h (300 rpm). The free peptide was precipitated using cold diisopropyl ether, solubilized in water and lyophilized. The synthesis was confirmed by MALDI-ToF/ToF mass spectrometry on a Bruker Autoflex® III SmartBeam spectrometer (Bruker Daltonics®, Germany). Purification was performed at room temperature on a RP-HPLC Pro Star 315 system from Varian® (Santa Clara, USA). This step was carried out using a semipreparative C18 Waters Bondapack® (7.8 × 300 mm) column at a flow rate of 2.0 mL⋅min−1. Elution was performed with a gradient of water containing 0.1% (v:v) TFA (solvent A) and acetonitrile containing TFA 0.08% (v:v) (solvent B). The peptide was detected at 214 and 280 nm. The identity of purified EcPis-4s was confirmed by MALDI-ToF mass spectrometry using a Bruker Autoflex® III Smart Beam spectrometer (Bruker Daltonics®, Germany) with a reflected and positive mode, employing α-cyano-4-hydroxycinnamic acid as matrix.

LUVs were prepared with the phospholipids 1-palmitoyl-2-oleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine (POPC) and 1-palmitoyl-2-oleoyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphoglycerol (POPG) to be used as model of bacterial membranes in circular dichroism and fluorescence dye release experiments. The proper amounts of POPC and POPG lipids (3:1, mol:mol) were transferred to a glass tube and co-solubilized with chloroform at room temperature. The organic solvent of the lipid suspension was removed in a rotary evaporator at 40 °C resulting in a lipid film, which was hydrated with 10 mM aqueous phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) containing 100 mM NaCl to obtain a 2 mM stock solution. Large multilamellar vesicles (LMV) were then formed by vigorous vortexing the solution, which was subsequently subjected to eight freeze-thawing cycles (8 cycles) - alternating a liquid nitrogen and a 40 °C water baths. Extrusion was then performed at room temperature with a mini-extruder (Avanti Polar Lipids, Inc.) through 8 steps by using membranes of polycarbonate Nucleopore Track-Etch filters (Whatman-GE Healthcare) to obtain 100 nm sized LUV.

For the calcein release experiments, free calcein was removed by gel filtration through a Sephadex G50 small column equilibrated with a 10 mM aqueous phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) containing 100 mM NaCl. Chromatography was performed at room temperature, just before the leakage experiments. The total phospholipid content of the eluted LUVs was determined by a colorimetric method as described in the literature [24].
The obtained LUVs were collected and immediately used in the circular dichroism and calcein release assays.

The methodology for preparing carbon dots was based on the dehydration and oxidation reactions of cotton cellulose as described elsewhere [25–27] First, 5 g of cotton were carefully dispersed in 20 mL of concentrated H2SO4 at 80 °C. Then, the obtained carbon material was redispersed in 125 mL of HNO3 (2 M) and refluxed during 8h – reaction time needed to the suspension change its color from black to red-brown. Thereafter, the suspension was neutralized with Na2CO3 and dialyzed until constant conductivity. The conductivity of the suspension reached equilibrium at 27 μScm−1, and the obtained concentration of the carbon dots suspension was 2.26 g/L.

Surface modification reaction of the CD was carried out based on the concentration of surface oxygenated functional groups. 50 mg of carbon dots were mixed with 0.04275g of EDAC at 45 °C under stirring. After 30 min of reaction, 0.317g of OxymaPure® was added to the solution, and the reaction was performed at 45 °C under stirring for 3 h to obtain the “activated CD”. Finally, 1.0 mL of an aqueous solution of ecPis-4s peptide at 0.65 mg mL−1, previously quantified by spectroscopic determination of tryptophan residue [28], was added to the solution containing the activated CD in a stepwise manner (20 aliquots of 50 μL at each 10 min), and the reaction proceeded for 2 h. The obtained suspension of functionalized CD (CD@ecPis-4s) was dialyzed until constant conductivity. The amount of peptide immobilized onto the CD nanoparticles was evaluated based on the concentration of free peptide in the reaction solution and in the residual aqueous solution obtained from the dialysis. Both peptide contents were spectroscopically determined using the extinction coefficient of Trp and the difference between the found values was used to estimate the functionalization degree of peptide in CD nanoparticles. Finally, the CD@ecPis-4s solution was lyophilized, the dry material was redispersed in DCM, and filtered to remove the excess reagents and reaction by-products.

Digital images of carbon dots suspensions were firstly obtained under ambient light conditions and subsequently in the presence of UV light with maximum emission at 360 nm. The morphology and particle size of CD and CD@ecPis-4s were determined by Atomic Force Microscopy. A drop of the obtained CD suspension was deposited on a mica plate freshly cleaved and the experiment was performed on a SPM-9700 Shimadzu instrument (Maryland, USA).

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to characterize the CD obtained before and after functionalization. The spectra were recorded on a Varian 640-IR FT-IR spectrophotometer (Santa Clara, CA, USA) equipped with an attenuated total reflectance accessory (ATR, Pike Technologies, GladiATR model). The spectra were recorded with 32 accumulations from 4000 to 400 cm−1 using an 8 cm−1 step resolution.

For the NMR experiments, an aqueous solution of free peptide as well as CD and CD@ecPis-4s suspensions were prepared in a mixture of H2O/D2O (90/10, v/v) containing 10 mM 2,2-dimethyl-2-silapentane sulfonate (DSS) as internal reference. The experiments were performed at 25 °C on a Bruker® Fourier 300 NMR spectrometer (Fallanden, Switzerland) operating at 300.18 MHz for 1H. 1H experiments were acquired using composite pulses and pre-saturation to achieve water suppression (zgcppr pulse program). The spectra were recorded with 512 scans of 8,192 points for a spectral width 6103.52 Hz.

The zeta potential (ζ) of the nanoparticle suspensions were determined at pH 6.7 using a Zetasizer Nano ZS equipment (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK). All potentiometric titration curves were measured at 25 °C using an automatic titrator Titroline 7000 (SCHOTT, Singapore), according to the methodology described elsewhere [29].

The UV–Vis spectra were obtained on a spectrophotometer Cary 50 (Varian), using 1.0 cm quartz cells. The fluorescence spectra were obtained using the SpectraMax equipment (Molecular Devices). The quantum fluorescence yields were determined according to previously reported procedures, using quinine sulfate (0.5 M H2SO4) as standard [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biophysical studies in the presence of anionic LUVs
ce:section.ce:para: The conformational preferences of ecPis-4s (50 μmol L−1), CD (120 mg L−1) and CD@ecPis-4s (120 mg L−1) were investigated in 10 mM aqueous phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) and in the presence of 200 mmol L−1 POPC:POPG (3:1, mol:mol) LUVs. Circular dichroism spectra of the buffer and LUVs solutions were collected as backgrounds and subtracted from the respective spectra of the samples under investigation. All spectra were recorded at 25 °C on a JASCO J-815spectrometer coupled to a temperature control system (Peltier Jasco model PTC-423L, Jasco Inc, Easton, MD). The average spectra of eight scans were acquired from 190 to 260 nm with 100 nm min−1 scan speed and 4 s response time. The bandwidth was 1 nm and the ellipticity reading was performed at every 0.2 nm. All spectra were collected in a 1 mm optical path quartz cuvette.

Aliquots of calcein-loaded LUVs solution (150 μL) were added to polystyrene microplates (128 × 86 × 14.5 mm) for fluorescence emission measurement in medium containing 50 μL of ecPis-4s (25 mmol L−1), CD (60 mg L−1) and CD@ecPis-4s (60 mg L−1) in the same buffer used for the Sephadex-G25 column elution. The increase of calcein fluorescence as a function of time at 25 °C was continuously recorded in Spectra Max® Paradigm detection platform (Molecular Devices, LLC,Sunnyvale, US) using λex = 490 nm and λem = 515 nm. The total calcein fluorescence (maximum released) was determined by addition of 50 μL of 10% (v:v) Triton-X 100 after 32 min of experimental time. The percentage of calcein released (% calcein) was determined according to eq. (1),

The antimicrobial activities of free EcPis-4s, CD and CD@ecPis-4s were investigated against four bacteria species from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC- Rockville ® MD, USA): Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC29213), Escherichia coli (ATCC25922), Streptococcus agalactiae (ATCC29313), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC27853). The methodology of sensitivity tests to antimicrobial agents by dilution for aerobic growth bacteria was used as a reference (ANVISA, 2003; NCCLS, 2012). Initially, the bacterial cells used in the experiments were obtained from the standardization of the bacterial suspension as described in the CLSI manual (2006). Bacterial colonies were isolated from culture inoculated 24 h in the BHI solid medium. Then, the colonies were removed and resuspended in saline solution (0.09% m/V) until the turbidity that corresponds to the tube suspension to 0.5 on the McFarland scale was reached. The concentration of microorganisms was verified by spectrophotometric reading at 620 nm, where the absorbance range should be between 0.08 and 0.1, thus resulting in a suspension containing approximately 1–2 × 108 CFU mL−1 (colony forming units/mL). After this, a dilution (1:10) was performed in Kasvi® Mueller-Hinton broth to obtain a suspension of 1.0 x 107 CFU mL−1 and 50 μL was added to the wells of a microplate. An additional volume of 50 μL of the standardized bacterial suspension was added to each well. The conventional antibiotic chloramphenicol at 30 μg mL−1 (92.8 μmol L−1) was used as standard for controlling cell death (positive control). The microplates were subjected to agitation for 20 min on an orbital shaker (Fanem®) and then incubated at 37 °C. The concentrations of the samples used in the antibacterial activity assays are detailed in Table S1. After 22 h of incubation, 30 μL of a 0.01% (w/v) solution of 7-hydroxy-3H-phenoxazine-3-one-10-oxide dye - resazurine (Sigma®) was added to each well. The solutions were stirred for 20 min and re-incubated for additional 2 h in the absence of light, for color development. A change from blue to pink indicates the resazurin reduction and therefore bacterial growth. The MIC was defined as the lowest drug concentration that completely inhibits the bacteria growth in microdilution wells where no color change occurred. All assays were carried out in triplicate in three independent experiments.

The antioxidant capacity of the free peptide and nanoparticles were investigated through assays with 2,2-diphenyl-1-picryl-hydrazyl-hydrate (DPPH●). 244 μL of DPPH● (60 μM) were added to a microplate and the experiments were carried out based on previously published procedures [31,32,32]. Briefly, 6 μL aliquots were added into each dilution of the samples (diluted in methanol) and of the gallic acid standard, obtaining a final volume of 250 μL. A control sample, containing only DPPH● (60 μM) was also prepared. The plate was kept at room temperature and protected from light for a period of 30 min. After that, the absorbance of the reaction medium was read at 515 nm in a Spectramax® microplate reader (Molecular Devices).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots are obtained in two steps. Firstly, the cotton cellulose undergoes a complex dehydration, condensation and aromatization reactions in the presence of concentrated sulfuric acid, resulting in the carbon material [33,34]. The carbon dots are obtained in a second step by oxidation of the carbon material in HNO3 solution, which promotes the fragmentation of the graphitic-like structure (broken down into individual carbon nanoparticles) with the introduction of oxygenated functional groups [29,35].

The images of the CD suspensions under ambient light and UV radiation (λ max = 360 nm) are shown in Fig. 1 . Both solutions present colors typically observed for carbon dots suspensions. Whereas a reddish-yellow suspension is observed under the influence of ambient light, the incident UV radiation turns it into green due the emission resulted from the UV absorption of the CD.

AFM images confirmed the obtainment of the nanoparticles (Fig. 2 a). An average particle size of approximately 4 nm is indicated from various measurements of the height of the isolated nanoparticles. This result is similar to other CD nanoparticles reported in the literature, which had their average sizes also confirmed by TEM [27,29].

The presence of oxygenated functional groups in the CD was investigated by FTIR spectroscopy and potentiometric titration. Fig. 2 (paniels b and c) shows the FTIR spectra obtained for the precursor (cotton cellulose) and CD nanostructures.

In the spectrum of cellulose (Fig. 2b), the band at 3390 cm−1 is attributed to axial O–H stretches (νOH), the bands centered at 2900 and 1431 cm−1 are characteristic of νC-H stretches and deformation of -CH2- groups, respectively. In addition, the bands near 1100 cm−1 are attributed to the saccharide structure [36]. After the reactions in acidic media, the typical bands of cellulose, especially of the saccharide structure, are no longer observed in the spectrum of the CD (Fig. 2c). In its turn, a broad band between 3700 and 2500 cm−1 is observed in the spectrum of CD, which is mainly derived from νOH of carboxyl groups involved in hydrogen bonds. The presence of overlapped broad bands at 1500 and 1850 cm−1 (centered at 1560 cm−1) is characteristic of a complex surface composed of aromatic structures along with absorptions of different carbonyl functional groups – as also indicated by a shoulder at about 1730 cm−1. Besides, a band at 1362 cm−1 indicates the presence of nitro groups (-NO2), which are a consequence of the use of HNO3 in the preparation of the CD. Finally, the ether functional groups are characterized by the C–O absorptions around 1050 cm−1 – such groups are commonly derived from cellulose dehydration reactions.

The quantification of the surface functional groups was performed by an acid-base titration, as many of these groups are carboxylic acid and phenolic functions, which present Brønsted acidity. Table S2 shows the results obtained after processing the data using a nonlinear regression fit [37].

The overall surface concentration of acid functional groups of the obtained CD nanoparticles is 4.55 mmol g−1. In the case of carbon materials, a pK a range between 2.0 and 6.0 is typically assigned to carboxylic groups, whereas a pK a range between 6.0 and 8.0 is attributed to acidic groups derived from the hydrolysis of cyclic esters (lactones) under the influence of acidic or basic media. In its turn, pK as greater than 9.0 are usually attributed to phenolic groups [37]. Around 60% of the total acidic functional groups (2.73 mmol g−1) correspond to the pK a characteristic to carboxylic groups, which are mainly responsible for the observed zeta potential of −50.5 mV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of the carbon dots with ecPis-4s
ce:section.ce:para: Several methods for surface modification of carbon dots have been proposed, including silylation, esterification, amide coupling reactions, and others, such as sulfonylation and copolymerization [38]. Structural modifications with amide linkages are typically carried out via activated acyl compounds [39]. The main advantage of amidation of CD nanoparticles is the improved quantum fluorescence yield caused by the consumption of carboxylic functional groups. The presence of excessive carboxyl groups on the surface of the nanostructures often results in emissive traps, which may affect their fluorescence properties [38].

Amide coupling reactions were previously used in some cases to obtain carbon dots modified with the antimicrobial glycopeptide Vancomycin. The authors have shown the fluorescence quenching of the carbon dots due to the binding of the peptide to the cell wall of the gram-positive bacterium via hydrogen bond interactions [40].

In the present work, the carbon nanostructures were functionalized by the formation of an amide bond between the amino groups of the ecPis-4s peptide and the carboxyl groups present on the surface of CD [10]. EcPis-4s was chosen due the high net charge (+7) which allow a well-stablished electrostatic interaction with anionic LUVs as well as the presence of the Trp residue in the central portion of the primary sequence of the peptide in order to provide the quantification of bound peptide to the CD nanoparticles. First, the ecPis-4s peptide was successfully obtained through solid-phase synthesis, as confirmed by the [M + H]+ signal at m/z 2607.9 in the respective mass spectrum (Fig. S1 - Supporting Material; theoretical [M + H]+ monoisotopic mass 2607.5). Thereafter, the carboxyl groups of CD were activated via reaction with EDAC, which is a water-soluble carbodiimide used as a carboxyl activating agent for coupling reactions of primary amines often employed in combination with N-hydroxysuccinimide for the immobilization of biomolecules [41]. (Scheme 1 supplementary information) shows the step of carboxyl group activation and the amide bond formation after addition of the peptide. The first step consists of carboxyl activation with EDAC to form a reactive O-acylisourea intermediate (1). Subsequently, the ethyl(hydroxyimino)cyanoacetate reagent (oxyma) added to the reaction medium leads to an addition/elimination reaction of the carbonyl group of the O-acylisourea intermediate, originating a second pre-formed active ester intermediate (2). The last step is characterized by the nucleophilic attack of the amino group of the ecPis-4s N-terminus to the pre-formed active ester intermediate 2 (Scheme 2 - supplementary information), which leads to the amide bond link between the CD and the peptide chain. The amount of free peptide before and after the coupling reaction between CD and ecPis-4s were determined by extinction coefficient of Trp and the functionalization degree of peptide in the nanoparticle determined (0.42 mmol g−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the carbon dots containing ecPis-4s
ce:section.ce:para: The first evidence of CD functionalization was obtained from the zeta potential measurements of the nanoparticles before and after functionalization with the peptide. The zeta potential of the CD increases from −50.5 mV to −23.4 mV due the presence of cationic residues in primary structure of ecPis-4s (net charge +7). Fig. 3 shows the FTIR spectra collected for the CD, CD@ecPis-4s and free ecPis-4s. In the ecPis-4s spectrum, the bands at 1647 and 1529 cm−1 are related to the amide I (νC=O) and amide II (δN-H) stretchings, respectively. The bands comprised between 1200 and 1080 cm−1 are attributed to the axial deformation of C–O and C–N characteristic of peptide bonds. In the spectrum of the CD@ecPis-4s, bands of both peptide and CD are noted. The amide group which is responsible for the binding of the peptide on the CD surface is characterized by the bands at 1638 and 1560 cm−1. In addition, the narrowing of the absorption band at 3340 cm−1 in the spectrum of CD@ecPis-4s when compared to the spectrum of CD indicates a reduced number of carboxylic acid groups, which is expected upon peptide binding to the nanoparticle surface.

Fig. 5 shows the UV–Vis spectra obtained for the CD, CD@ecPis-4s and ecPis-4s at different concentrations. The optical properties of the carbon nanostructures are in general highly complex because of the dependence of the π structure of the basal planes (core) and functional groups, mainly the oxygenated ones.

The CD presented optical absorption in the UV region, which extends up to approximately 500 nm. It is possible to observe a slight shoulder near 300 nm, which has been assigned to the n→π* transitions of the CO chromophore of different functional groups within CD structures. In addition, the strong absorption near 250 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition, which is characteristic of CC bonds. The absorptivities (ε) measured at 290 and 220 nm are 9.3 ± 0.2 and 16.9 ± 0.4 L g−1 cm−1 at, respectively. The UV spectra of the ecPis-4s peptide reveal the presence of a band near 290 nm, mainly related to the absorbance of the peptide backbone, although there are also contributions from the aromatic side chains of the phenylalanine and tryptophan residues. An absorptivity of 1.14 ± 0.68 L g−1 cm−1 was measured at 290 nm. The spectra of CD@ecPis-4s are quite similar to those of the carbon dots, since the absorptivity of the functional groups of the peptide is significantly lesser when compared to the CD. Nevertheless, higher absorptivity at 290 nm (10.9 ± 2.3 L g−1 cm−1) is noticed when the UV spectra of CD@ecPis-4s are compared to the respective spectra of CD, due to the absorption of the peptide chain covalently bound to the nanostructures.

Fig. 6 presents the photoluminescence spectra of CD and CD@ecPis-4s samples obtained by excitation at wavelengths from 360 to 400 nm with 20 nm intervals. The emission wavelength maximum shift to red (from 440 nm to ∼460 nm) with the increase of the excitation wavelengths (from 360 nm to 400 nm) reveals an excitation-dependent emission profile of the carbon dots. The emission excitation wavelength-dependence is related to different particle sizes (optical selection of nanoparticles and quantum confinement effect) or distributions of emission sites on the surface of the carbon dots. The groups present on CD surface may introduce trapping states with different energy levels [43].

The fluorescence quantum yield of the CD and CD@ecPis-4s were estimated using the quinine sulfate standard and the values obtained for the CD and CD@ecPis-4s were 0.92% and 1.06%, respectively. This fluorescence quantum yield in the peptide-decorated nanoparticle is related to new chromophore groups (Phe and Trp side chains), which were introduced on the surface of the carbon dots upon peptide binding [44]. Despite the slight increase in the quantum yield, this result shows that the optical properties, especially the fluorescence, of the carbon dots were not impaired even in the presence of the peptide (see supplementary material, Fig. S2). This is an important result since functionalization of carbon dots with antimicrobial peptides may contribute to the development of new probes for infectious microbial agents [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biophysical studies in anionic mimetic membrane
ce:section.ce:para: Cationic antimicrobial peptides as ecPis-4s establish strong interaction with bacterial membranes [45]. Therefore conformational and lytic activity of ecPis-4s, CD and CD@ecPis-4s were performed in the presence of anionic vesicles in order to mimic the phospholipid composition and mechanics of the bacterial membrane [46]. To directly compare helicity degrees as well as the dye leakage percentages of the CD@ecPis-4s nanoparticles with the free ecPis-4s, the degree of nanoparticle functionalization with the peptide (0.42 mmol g−1) was taken into account.

Secondary structural preferences of the free and nanoparticle-bound ecPis-4s peptide were investigated by circular dichroism spectroscopy in 10 mM aqueous phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) and in the presence of POPC:POPG (3:1 mol/mol) vesicles [47]. Fig. 7 a shows the spectral profiles obtained for the peptide ecPis4s, CD and CD@ecPis-4s in the absence and presence of anionic LUVs. In the presence of anionic LUVs, the spectrum of free ecPis-4s shows a profile typical of α-helical conformations, which is characterized by intense molar ellipiticities of two minima near 208 nm and 222 nm and of a maximum near 193 nm. Several antimicrobial peptides exhibit clear helical profiles in the presence of mimetic membrane environments, as they adopt the active antimicrobial conformation [48–51]. On the contrary, circular dichroism spectra of linear antimicrobial peptides in water or aqueous buffers tend to reveal random coil conformations and are characterized by an intense minimum near ∼198 nm. Interestingly, the spectrum of ecPis-4s in aqueous buffer shows a distinct profile, as the entire spectral window shows relatively lower molar ellipticities in absolute values, but it is characterized by a minimum near 208 nm and a flat minimum region between 220 and 225 nm. This result suggests that the free peptide may acquire a small content of α-helix conformation even in aqueous medium. As expected, the CD does not present absorption signals of circular dichroism in both media. Interestingly, the spectrum of CD@ecPis-4s suspended in phosphate buffer solution presents a positive absorption at wavelengths <200 nm and a negative band centered at 214 nm, which is indicative of β-sheet conformations [9]. This finding suggests polypeptide interchain interactions within the nanostructure, as previously observed from fibrous alumina nanoparticles decorated with antimicrobial peptides [9]. Nevertheless, in the presence of LUVs, the polypeptide chains of CD@ecPis-4s adopts a well-defined α-helical conformation, confirming the interaction of the nanoparticle-bound peptide chains with the anionic phospholipid membranes [52]. As peptide structure in membrane environment is a key feature to antimicrobial activity, this result indicate that the peptide-conjugated nanoparticles may present antimicrobial properties, similarly to ecPis-4s. To our knowledge, this is the first investigation of peptide moiety conformational preferences of peptide-decorated carbon dots.

Calcein release studies were conducted for free ecPis-4s, CD and CD@ecPis-4s in the presence of POPC:POPG (3:1,mol:mol) LUVs (Fig. 7b). The calcein leakage from LUVs with addition of pure CD is virtually negligible during the experimental time (32 min). On the other hand, it is noticed that both ecPis-4s and CD@ecPis-4s immediately promote pronounced dye releases from the calcein-loaded vesicles. Interestingly, after 5 min of injection, a relatively smaller percentage of vesicle leakage is induced by the CD@ecPis-4s (∼30%) in comparison to free ecPis-4s (∼60%), indicating a slower calcein release kinetics for CD@ecPis-4s. However, both species achieve almost the same percentage of leakage at 30 min (around 90%), which indicates that both the free and the CD-bound peptide are able to exert lytic activity on the anionic phospholipid bilayers.

The development of surface functionalization methods or coatings that inhibit the growth of infectious microorganisms is an interesting strategy to obtain new materials for applications in the food and medical-hospital industry [53,54]. As proved by circular dichroism spectroscopy, nanoparticle-bound ecPis-4s retains its ability to interact with anionic phospholipid membrane as it acquires a high degree of α-helical conformation, similar to the free peptide. Therefore, the antimicrobial activity of the CD, free ecPis–4s and the CD@ecPis-4s were investigated against gram-negative E. coli and P. aeruginosa as well as against gram-positive S. aureus bacterial and S. agalactiae strains (Fig. 8 ).

As expected, ecPis-4s shows antibacterial activity against all bacterial strains tested, with similar values to the previously observed by Zhuang et al. (2017) for E. coli (15 μmol L−1) and S. aureus (20 μmol L−1) [21]. The MIC values are about 2–4 times smaller than the positive control (chloramphenicol). Nevertheless, non-functionalized CD showed virtually no activity even at relatively higher concentrations (up to 400.8 mg L−1). On the other hand, the CD@ecPis-4s nanoparticles were highly active against the tested strains, indicating that the presence of ecPis-4s peptide chain is responsible for the antibacterial activity of the peptide-CD nanostructures. Although Dong et al. also confirmed the antimicrobial activity of 2,2’-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) (EDA)-functionalized CD against gram-negative E.coli with a MIC of 64 μg/mL [55], this is the first time that the antimicrobial activity of a peptide-decorated CD is reported in the literature. Interestingly, CD@ecPis-4s showed a MIC twice as small against S. aureus and S. agalactiae strains when compared to the free peptide, confirming that ecPis-4s retain its ability to interact with bacterial membranes even bound to the CD surface as observed in the circular dichroism and fluorescence dye release experiments. These findings suggest that the presence of carbon nanoparticles has a positive impact on the mechanism of peptide-interaction for gram-positive cells, increasing the inhibitory action. Several cationic antimicrobial peptides, as ecPis-4s self-aggregate in the surface membrane lead a disruption process after achieve a critical concentration [56,57]. When peptide-decorated nanoparticle achieves the bacterial cell, several peptide molecules interact simultaneously with the membrane. The high local concentration of peptide in the phospholipid bilayer, contributing to the achieve the critical peptide concentration needed for the membrane disruption. Such effect was proposed by Torres et al. [9], which observed similar results using alumina nanoparticles functionalized with [Trz-β-A34] -DD K, as the peptide-decorated alumina nanoparticles have strong inhibitory action for E. coli, S. aureus, and S Typhimurium when compared to the free peptide.

Antioxidant peptides have attracted great interest from researchers due to their potential applications in the development of health-promoting foods and in maintaining the quality and safety of food products, which is of major interest to the food industry [58]. The antioxidant properties of ecPi-4s, CD and CD@ ecPi-4s were determined by the in vitro free radicals scavenging activity using DPPH● and the results a presented based on the percentage of radical inhibition in Table S3. However, at the investigated concentrations neither the peptide nor the CD species presented values compatible with the inhibition of 50% of the DPPH● radicals. In other words, in the evaluated concentrations the compounds did not present antioxidant activity in the time interval of 30 min. Nevertheless, some authors have shown the potential of applying carbon dots as oxidizing agents [16]. Zhang et al. showed a significant decrease in the concentration of DPPH● with the increase in the concentration of carbon dots [59]. In the present work, we have opted for using nanoparticles with a high concentration of oxygenated functional groups, especially carboxylic acids, i.e., very oxidized CD species were produced to proceed with the functionalization with ecPis-4s. The relatively low radical scavenging capacities indicate that these oxygenated functional groups harmed the antioxidant activity of the CD materials. Regarding the peptide, despite the presence of amino acid residues with the potential to act as oxidizing agents, other variables such as conformation and sequence are decisive for effective action [58].

Carbon dots were initially prepared from dehydration/oxidation reactions of cellulose and the carboxyl groups activated with EDAC and ECHA in an aqueous medium to provide the covalent binding of the antimicrobial peptide ecPis-4s. The peptide-decorated carbon dots were chemically characterized by FTIR and 1H NMR and spectroscopies. The absorption and emission electron spectroscopy showed that functionalization with peptide does not alter the optical properties of the CD, i.e., molar absorptivity and quantum fluorescence yield. In addition, the covalently bound peptide retains its ability to interact with membranes, since it presents active α-helical conformation and lytic activity in the presence of anionic phospholipid vesicles comparable to the free peptide. As a result, the nanobiostructures showed inhibitory activity in vitro against both S. aureus and E. coli. No significant antioxidant activity was observed for ecPis-4s and the prepared nanomaterials at the investigated concentrations and time interval. Finally, we believe that the functionalized nanoparticles present suitable surface properties for dispersion in different hydrophilic polymeric matrices, especially biopolymers, contributing to the development of new nanocomposites with fluorescent, antimicrobial and improved mechanical properties. Although some advances in the optical properties could be achieved, namely quantum fluorescence yield, the strategy used here may contribute to the future development of antimicrobial materials and surfaces with potential for biomedical applications as bacterial contamination monitoring.
